data.title: Q3 2025 China Update: At BYD, Domestic Problems Dominate
data.description:
Overview

The Chinese car market is at a historic high and is larger by far than any other single market. In 2025 passenger vehicle sales (including exports) will surpass 25 million; add in commercial vehicles, and it's a 30 million unit market, so almost double the size of the US or the EU markets. While it's the best of times, it's also the worst of times: profits have been falling the past five years, even as sales exploded. BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF) (OTCPK:BYDDY) is no exception: profits in 2025Q3 are down 33% YoY, while revenue is only down 3% (source: SA news).

The key question for investors is whether this is temporary. To provide an answer, first I trace the industry context and then look in more detail at 2025Q3, using the database of the Chinese passenger vehicle market I have compiled over the last several years. Then I provide an overview of BYD as a firm, noting that we have little information on significant slices of their businesses. I then focus on their largest slice, domestic passenger vehicle sales. I argue that they are now a mature firm, and so BYD has to focus on renewing rather than extending their product line, all in the context of a highly competitive industry that requires them to invest in new technologies. Exports are growing rapidly but are so far insufficient to keep factories fully utilized. While global markets provide the potential for continued growth, that will come as much from local production in target markets, and success is neither quick nor certain.

For the next several years, BYD’s top and bottom line will thus be dominated by domestic performance. Key to improving that will be strengthening their brands to improve pricing power. That will require several years, and BYD continues to face rivals doing the same. Meanwhile, I do not believe investors should factor in top- or bottom-line growth. Even though the Thursday, Nov. 20th price of $12.26 was almost 40% below the 12-month peak, I believe that BYD remains slightly overpriced and so rate it a “sell.”

The Auto Industry Context

First, over the past decade, booming sales stimulated new entry, both private and public. The latter was a function of China's political economy, under which senior provincial and municipal government officials used the industry to bolster their growth credentials. At the same time, that same political economy slowed the restructuring and/or exit of not just the state-owned enterprises (SOEs) and also allowed perennial money-losers such as NIO (NIO)(OTCPK:NIOIF) to keep raising fresh money. So there's capacity to produce 40-50 million vehicles, roughly twice what the domestic market can absorb. The resulting discounting hurts everyone, both the strong with good capacity utilization, such as BYD and Tesla (TSLA), and the weak.

Second, the modern industry began in 2001, when GM launched the Buick Sail. That forced VW and subsequent entrants to match current global standards rather than recycle stale products. That was a quarter century ago. Over that timespan the older players—the big government-owned producers and private firms such as BYD, Geely, Chery, and Great Wall—beefed up their product lineups. As mature producers, they now must devote resources to preserving the sales of existing products and keeping up with improvements across the industry in drivetrains and styling, rather than expanding their footprint and sales with new products. Just this past week Chery launched their 5th generation Tiggo (瑞虎); the Landian E5 is now in its 3rd iteration (with its maker Sokon spun off from Dongfeng and now producing Huawei HIMA models). In October 2025 some 450 models had sales over 250 units, and another 219 models sold between 1 and 249 units. In the US market, the standard is a 4-year replacement cycle. With so much competition, Chinese firms can't afford to wait 3 years—so far in 2025 I’ve counted 177 announcements of new or fully refreshed models and another 173 announcements of minor updates. That drives up costs as well as pushing down prices.

China's Auto Market, 2025Q3

There are signs that the EV market is peaking; going forward, the government's "new for old" incentives, for which 10 million car purchasers have applied so far in 2025, will be phased out, as will the sales tax exemption for EVs. Now when sales crashed after cash subsidies for EVs were initially phased out, the government backtracked. I would not be surprised if sales fall in early 2026, and the government responds with some form of sales incentives. After all, car sales have been one of the bright spots for the economy. Still, I expect sales growth to moderate.

Sales

It's really been a great two years, with retail volumes blowing past the previous peaks of 2018 and 2021. Wholesale shipments have done even better, as the industry has shifted from being a net importer in 2019 to surpassing Germany and Japan as exporters. (See the middle chart.)

Now sales are not falling, but they are rising slowly relative to last year, and as the chart above shows, winter is normally the strongest sales period. The industry, though, needs more than modest growth, given that firms have been slow to restructure. Not that there hasn't been an exit—since I started tracking monthly model-level sales in January 2020 some 27 makers have disappeared, including the collapse at the end of 2024 of the once high-flying Hozon (the Neta 哪吒 brand—see Automotive News). In addition, another 8 companies sold under 1,000 vehicles in October, and I expect some to soon quit the industry. Exit has been offset by new entry and new capacity additions at BYD, Nio, Xiaomi, the Huawei HIMA alliance, and a host of others. It should be no surprise that profits crashed.

Finally, the transition from ICEs to EVs/PHEVs continues, but among new energy vehicles (NEVs), the sales of PHEVs have stalled. The recent increase in the NEV share has been helped by "New for Old" policies that encourage the scrapping of older ICEs for new NEVs. However, the national NEV share is converging to the levels in mature markets such as Shanghai, which has long encouraged NEV sales through favorable license plate allocations. At almost 60% of the market, I believe that the EV/PHEV share will peak, so NEV producers will no longer be able to ride a rising tide. That’s been important for BYD, which no longer produces pure ICEs.

Both slower growth and price pressures weigh on BYD. BYD reduced prices on 22 models in May 2025, but due to similar moves across the industry, that failed to stimulate sales. BYD also has to focus on updating both drivetrains and vehicles, and not just expanding their product lineup. Of course, expanding offerings naturally leads to increasing sales (as long as new models don't flop!). Unfortunately for BYD, renewing models costs just as much money as launching new products but at best preserves sales.

BYD

As for BYD, Berkshire Hathaway was an early investor (from 2008) but began unwinding its position in 2022 and as of March 31, 2025, has sold its entire holding (source: Politico plus multiple SA articles). Let's see why.

Overview of BYD and its business lines

BYD began as a battery manufacturer; its founder, Wang Chuanfu, is a metallurgical engineer who used his training to work for a battery maker and then founded his own firm to produce lithium batteries for cell phones and other portable devices. BYD subsequently moved into electric buses and finally passenger vehicles, along the way becoming the world's second-biggest battery producer. It also built a robust contract assembly business for cell phones and other portable devices. (See BYD’s annual reports and Wikipedia for details.) They are now growing their energy storage business—BYD has a 30 GWh sodium-ion battery plant under construction, as the battery industry starts to move to non-lithium chemistries. I believe Chinese battery producers such as CATL and BYD are now positioned to dominate the global energy storage market, not firms such as Tesla, which buy batteries from others.

Handsets

BYD's handset business has grown at about the same rate as their vehicle & battery business but has been slightly more profitable. (See BYD’s financial reports for details.) I do not see this as a growth business, and so do not try to analyze it.

Batteries

I estimate that about 60% of BYD's battery production is used internally for BYD vehicles, using their share of the vehicle battery market of 145 GWh (data are from SNE Research/Gasgoo for Jan-Sep 2025 global battery sales). The global leader, CATL, was at 297 GWh. Using my database, which includes vehicle unit sales and battery pack sizes, I estimate BYD's Jan-Sep 2025 internal consumption at 92 MWh, or about 60% of their battery production, implying external sales of 40%. With CATL revenue for that period of $40 billion, I estimate BYD external battery revenue of $40 billion x 40% x 145 GWh / 297 GWh, or just under $8 billion. That's about 10% of BYD’s Jan-Sep revenue.

Summary

Automotive revenue is thus about 70% of total revenue, handsets 20%, and external battery sales 10%. BYD is a big company (data below are from Seeking Alpha’s “Financials” tab), about the size of BMW but with half the profits (BMW Q3 revenue profits were $26.6 billion and $2.7 billion). Both are smaller in revenue than Ford, with revenue at US$47.2 billion (profits $1.6 billion) and GM with revenue at $44.3 billion (profits $2.8 billion). Like those three, BYD is now a mature firm in its home market, no longer presenting a growth story for either revenue or profits. With slow growth of the domestic market, BYD’s growth and profits depend on exports and overseas production, potentially supplemented by their relatively new energy storage business, for which I have found no details.

BYD’s Domestic Auto Business

BYD’s growth has been nothing short of incredible. In January 2020 it was barely a player in the Chinese market. In October it slightly outsold the Geely group (which includes Lynk & Co. and Polestar) to remain the largest company in the domestic market.

BYD’s Domestic Strategic Challenge: Branding

As argued above, BYD faces a tough market. I believe this also reflects a deeper strategic challenge. BYD was founded to produce cell phone batteries, and only later expanded into buses and eventually passenger vehicles. As I see it, BYD is an engineering powerhouse (100,000 technical staff) that is dominated by an engineering mindset. It lacks a charismatic car guy as its public face. Its management is systematic; BYD dutifully hired experienced stylists, and produces (to my eyes) beautifully styled, high-quality vehicles that are at the industry's technological forefront. As the global #2 in the LFP (lithium-iron-phosphorus) batteries that dominate NEVs in the Chinese market, BYD's vertical integration gives them lower battery costs than any other car company, and it now has sufficient production volume to spread fixed costs. It may be the global industry’s lowest-cost producer, and is certainly the lowest-cost EV producer, with designs that are now better than Tesla's.

Low costs are not enough. BMW is profitable because of its strong brand, not because of low costs. BYD's greatest challenge is creating stronger brand identities. Until it can do that, domestic profitability will not improve.

That's a work in progress, as it includes clarifying existing product lines. One issue is that BYD still has legacy models that do not cleanly fall into either the "Ocean" or the "Dynasty" series, its two mass-market distribution channels. One set of these is the BYD letter series, such as the D1, e6 and e7, which BYD sells to taxi companies and the DiDi ride-hailing service. Then there are 2 models named after naval ships (Corvette, Destroyer) that were the first of the Ocean series. BYD subsequently shifted to animal names (Seal, Dolphin, Sea Lion, and Sea Gull), all starting in Chinese with the 海 (ocean) character, so they are now a poor fit. Indeed, the Corvette has been dropped (with under 100 monthly sales since April 2025), but the Destroyer has been selling well, so BYD has not (yet) discontinued it. There is also some mixing, as BYD sells the two Song Plus versions (EV and DM-i PHEV) through the Ocean channel. BYD needs brand clarity, but it will take time to drop models and relaunch others to fit core marketing identities.

On the positive side, BYD has added two models this year to its Fangchengbao line of performance and off-road SUVs, and two models to its Yangwang ultra-luxury brand (with $100,000+ price tags). Those brands will hopefully add a wow factor attractive to car enthusiasts, and thereby help the more plebeian Dynasty and Ocean products. BYD also continues to add models to the Denza luxury brand, which started as a joint venture with Mercedes-Benz but is now wholly owned by BYD.

BYD's Full Model Lineup

As it expanded its lineup, BYD's top and bottom lines both grew rapidly. Today, BYD has low, mid, luxury (Denza), and ultra-luxury (Yangwang) business lines. They sell passenger vehicles ranging from subcompacts (the Seagull in the A0 segment) to sedans and cars in the compact (A segment), midsized (B segment, cf. the Tesla Model 3/Y), full-sized segment, and large (D SUV) segment. They even produce an A00 model, exclusive for export to the Japanese market, and the Shark pickup, again exclusively for export (as in China, pickups are a small-volume segment sold only in rural markets). Thus, in the domestic market, BYD now covers all bases with a national dealership network that includes all large cities in China’s 31 mainland provinces (车主之家 has sales data for 300+ cities).

Unlike other global car companies, BYD does not publish plant-level capacity data in its shareholder reports. I conservatively estimate their domestic production capacity at 5.6 million units (core data here from 2022 to which I added announcements of expansions). I expect domestic sales of under 3.5 million units in 2025. Exports are thus critical for maintaining a profitable level of capacity utilization. To that end, BYD now has 8 RO/RO car carriers, with October 2025 exports of 80,000 units accounting for 15% of unit shipments. Extrapolating from data through October, I estimate CY2025 exports of 920,000 units. That will give total vehicle shipments of 4.4 million units, implying 78% capacity utilization (and only a slight 75% if I use the larger AI-search-based estimate of capacity). In general, automotive production costs rise sharply when capacity utilization falls much below 80%, so in the face of continuing pressure on profits, BYD needs exports.

The Full-Line Dilemma

Before getting into more details, here is the composition of BYD's passenger vehicle sales, including all brands and exports.

The challenge is that with a full product lineup, models age out in styling, and volumes are high enough to suffer when used car prices fall. As technology progresses, they also become obsolete in their performance—and China now sets the pace for global automotive innovation. To keep up, BYD is now launching their 5th generation of PHEV drivetrains. And I do mean “keep up,” because rivals also have new and highly efficient drivetrains. One example is the PHEV drivetrains Geely purchases from its spinoff Aurobay. A second is Seres, with its internally produced H15R, which it produces for its Aito brand and for the Wenjie models produced as a member of Huawei's HIMA alliance.

For BYD, a company that was barely a footnote in the Chinese market in January 2020, to be launching its 5th generation drivetrain in 2025 is remarkable. That begs another superlative: the R&D and investment cost of keeping its lineup competitive is frighteningly large.

My reading is that BYD's advantage remains access to leading-edge batteries. Its engineering is equal to that of anyone else in the global auto industry, but engineering doesn't sell cars, and cost competitiveness doesn't equate to profitability—low-volume, high-cost luxury cars wouldn't exist if people were utilitarian in their transport choices. Let me repeat: BMW remains nicely profitable thanks to its brand, not to low costs.

Model Line details

Dynasty channel

The largest of BYD's lineup is the Dynasty series (Xia, Qin, Han, Tang, Song, and Yuan, all from the distant past), which is sold through (uh, you guessed it!) their Dynasty dealership network.

As can be seen, early on the Song series formed the core of BYD's Dynasty sales, but the Song models faded over time. Han vehicles came next, but they too now comprise a small share. The Qin series is now the largest, while the Yuan series is growing.

Growth came as the company rounded out those product lines, with both EV and PHEV versions (the latter generally have the DM-I suffix) and, for some models, performance (“-p”) versions. Going forward, BYD can no longer obtain growth from adding models, and instead will have to invest in updating existing products. I indicate that process for the Qin and Han lines, with arrows indicating updates. (See my May 2025 Seeking Alpha article “China Autos Q1/April Update: Tesla Stalls” for that argument, as well as my Substack on the “Full Model Lineup Dilemma.”)

Ocean channel

We see the same thing with their BYD’s Ocean series. It started with a single model, the compact segment Dolphin, to which they then added the succeeding Seal, Seagull, and Sea Lion families to fill out other segments, and paired EV and DM-I (PHEV) versions. But as should be apparent (at least if you click to enlarge!), sales ebb and flow. Why? – As above, individual models age out, and then get renewed. The graphs below provide a summary of Ocean model sales, and then details of the Seal and Sea Lion models. (I don’t include the Song Pro, which, as noted above, is sold through the Ocean dealership network but belongs to the Dynasty series.)

Let's look at the renewal process in more detail for the Seal and the Sea Lion series.

Now I won't detail each version; let me remain the one who suffers from information overload. What does stand out is that, once launched, a version has 12-18 months of sales before it fades, which in part reflects cannibalization from subsequent versions. For the Seal, we observe 4 separate waves in the roughly 3 years since the initial launch in Aug 2022, as (i) the Seal version lost traction, to be (ii) offset by the Seal 07, then (iii) the burst of sales from the Seal 06, and only partially offset the past 6 months (iv) by the PHEV version of the Seal 05 and the EV version of the Seal 06.

The Sea Lion is more recent, with the first version coming only in May 2024. Only with the Seal Lion 05 EV, launched in March 2025, did sales expand. But that version has already faded from its peak. The Seal Lion 06 EV and PHEV launched in July 2025, and appear to be hitting their stride only in the past 2 months.

In sum, without stronger branding, the individual variations of a model don't stay strong sellers for long. Creating strong brands takes time and money.

The Full Model Line Dilemma: Comparison with NIO

Of course this flow and ebb is not unique to BYD. As another example, NIO's top 4 sellers are relatively recent models: in 2024Q1 they accounted for only 21% of sales, but hit 53% in 2025Q1 and 72% in 2025Q3. However, they are also at the less expensive end of NIO's lineup—I estimate they comprised only 62% of Q3 revenue. (The third chart below displays the data from the least expensive model at the bottom to the most expensive on top.) Meanwhile, former top sellers such as the ES7, ES8 and ET7 have either been dropped altogether or are a trivial share of sales. The models that added up to 100% of sales in 2022 Q4 are only 26% of sales in 2025 Q3, and today are only a bit over half of the 2022 Q4 levels in total volume. Furthermore, to date, NIO has flopped in the European market, selling a modest 1,288 units in Jan-Sep 2024, and only 773 units in the first 9 months of 2025. Moving downmarket sells more cars and helps improve their capacity utilization, but less expensive cars do not make a commensurate improvement to NIO’s top and bottom lines, and they continue to lose money.

Conclusion

BYD is no longer growing either its top or bottom line. Renewing its existing products will account for a growing share of their investment in their domestic car business. It does not help that rapid technical change is forcing upgrades to not just software but also drivetrains and battery packs. The other piece is building their brands, with investment in premium and super-premium models. (At BYD headquarters, where I had a lunch meeting this past June, visitors are welcome to inspect any car except the $230,000 Yangwang U9 sports car, which is roped off.) That may eventually create a halo effect, but the benefit will not be immediate. With a full model lineup, BYD needs to improve customer retention so that they can decrease their investment in renewing products—and stop discounting. The challenge is that everyone else in the industry is trying to do the same thing.

BYD competes on price, albeit with good designs, and a full array of options, including its “God’s Eye” driver assist software and sensor suite. Competing on price in its largest market does not present a compelling investment story, now that it can no longer grow by entering new niches.

Global Expansion

There is one development that could change BYD's position: exports and overseas production. Automotive News cites a Citi report forecasting 1.5 to 1.6 million sales outside China in 2026. In January-October they exported 750,000 vehicles, and with recent levels of 80,000 units a month, they can certainly hit 1.0 million next year. Consistent with that (as noted above), they now have a dedicated fleet of 8 RORO [roll-on, roll-off] car carriers, with capacities of 7,000-9,000 cars per trip. At 1.5 round trips a month, they have the capacity to export 1.2 million units.

I visited a BYD dealership in Manila in February 2025 – they have paired with the Ayala Group, a large family-run conglomerate that has years of experience running dealerships for Honda, VW and others. In the Philippines, BYD has 6 different models for sale (and more now being listed), and a months-long waitlist for everything. Their constraint is the number of cars allocated by BYD, not demand. Cars in the Philippines are all imports, and expensive. BYD is happy to price to market, which is far higher than the price inside China. As a low-cost producer, they face the same situation in other export markets: they earn excellent margins by pricing just below the competition.

The Philippines is a small market, but representative of many lower-income countries. Europe is much bigger. In Europe, BYD has been gradually expanding their dealership networks. This year (through September 2025), BYD has sold 120,000 cars, but sales are increasing rapidly. Their volume of nearly 25,000 units in September 2025 was over five times the level of September 2024. Both from a logistics and from a political economy perspective, however, expansion will increasingly be via local production. They have a plant with an initial capacity of 150,000 units slated to start production in Hungary in early 2026, and a plant under construction in Turkey of similar size, with target completion in late 2026.

BYD opened a plant in Thailand in July 2024 with a 150,000-vehicle annual capacity (source: CnEVPost), and a small (50K unit) plant in Uzbekistan designed with expansion in mind. They are also building a 150K plant in Indonesia, slated to start production in early 2026. Finally, their Brazil plant started production in summer 2025, and BYD already plans to double capacity to 300K units a year, with room at their production site to double yet again. As with other producers in Brazil, BYD has engineered their PHEVs to use biofuels; they’re good engineers.

Combine all of this, and BYD will continue to expand overseas. However, it will take time to hit volumes in any given market sufficient to achieve profitability, and there’s no guarantee that sales will be sufficient to make local production profitable. (Toyota and Hyundai both withdrew from the US market when initial sales efforts flopped, and Mazda, Suzuki, Mitsubishi, and Isuzu all closed or sold their US factories.) BYD’s main problem remains excess capacity, with weak pricing and little prospect of an expanding domestic market. Exports are a crutch, and their ability to supplement domestic sales will diminish as local production in key markets outside China increases.

My Bottom Line

I last covered BYD in February 2024 (see Disruption and EVs: Tesla, GM Wuling, and BYD in China). At that time, BYD was still growing rapidly, and saw no need to discount. It is no longer growing, despite discounting that has eroded margins, and is now spending money to renew existing products and not just to expand its product lineup. I have lowered my outlook accordingly.

In my opinion, BYD has upside potential, while Ford, GM, Toyota, and BMW do not, so it should carry a premium relative to other carmakers, who hover around a P/E of 10. However, I argue that BYD faces challenges that would mitigate against a recovery in domestic profits for at least the next 2 years. Profits from exports will likewise not be immediately significant, given the costs of setting up distribution networks in multiple markets. CapEx for building production facilities in Europe hits the bottom line before profits arrive, while local production partially substitutes for exports. BYD will, I believe, grow over time. However, the addition to the top line will come before any improvement in the bottom line, and it’s the bottom line that should concern investors.

What I bring to the analysis is my knowledge of the global auto industry, and my detailed tracking of China’s domestic vehicle market. Most analysts (see the SA summaries) are optimistic—indeed, 12 of the 13 SA articles since the start of August rate BYD as a “Buy” or “Strong Buy.” Only Eric Sprague rated it as a “Hold.”

I agree that BYD’s performance has been remarkable, but I argue against the consensus. BYD makes great cars—I've seen the product in person on the roads across China, as a DiDi ride-hail passenger, at BYD headquarters in Shenzhen, at dealerships in the Philippines, and at the US-based teardown specialist CareSoft. However, BYD does not make great profits, and I do not see that changing for several years. I think BYD will become a truly global player, but that will be a slow evolution, and as a now-mature company in their home market, they face the tough slog of constantly updating products in a market with severe overcapacity and pressures to keep prices low at the expense of profits. While I would be comfortable calling it a “buy” at a P/E of 16, that would be at $9, or 30% below the current level.

Bottom Line: While it's the best of times for EV sales, it's the worst of times for profits.

Data Note

I maintain a database of monthly model-level domestic sales, covering January 2020 through October 2025. My database covers over 1,100 models, including ones no longer sold from makers that have closed their doors since 2020. For each model, I have segment, price, drivetrain type, brand and firm, and for BYD data on their battery pack size. I began with data compiled by the Chinese Passenger Car Association and Gasgoo, but now rely on three other Chinese-language sources of monthly data. Each of those has slightly different coverage, so I collate to expand my coverage, check for consistency across sources, check drivetrain and segment information, and update (mostly lower!) prices periodically from car shopping sites.

My model-level data do not cover imports (6% of the market in January 2020, under 2.5% lately) or exports (recently about 20%). For those, I have brand- or firm-level data. Finally, my data do not include light commercial vehicles, though again I have aggregate data. That is less important than in the US as, for example, pickups are not a consumer vehicle in China—as noted above, BYD’s Shark is currently only made for export. Unfortunately it’s not available in the US, or I would be driving it instead of the Chevy Colorado pickup I leased in spring 2025.

I have been following the auto industry for over 40 years, and scan news on the Chinese market daily (I read Chinese and Japanese). My main China auto folder has 12 Chinese sites, such as Gasgoo (盖世汽车), CADA/CPCA (中国汽车流通协会•乘联分合) and the Morning Report (晨报) of 第一电动 that open when I turn on my computer every morning. As part of my research, I have been to roughly 150 automotive plants around the world. I visited 5 different carmakers in China in June-July 2025, including a meeting over lunch at BYD headquarters in Shenzhen.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Apple: More Catalysts Needed For Material Upside
data.description:
In late August, I provided an update on Apple Inc. (AAPL) and upgraded the stock to a hold, as there were signs that their business had hit an inflection point. As shown below, that update was quite timely, as the stock has gone on to advance by another ~18% as of this writing. Today, we'll review their latest earnings as well as discuss some key recent events that hold significant implications for the company's outlook.

In the below discussion, Apple's fundamentals are determined to be quite solid. Sales growth was decent in their Q4 and iPhone 17 strength is expected to power upcoming results. The reported Gemini deal and CEO succession plans could help to drive long-term shareholder value, but with the valuation having expanded since my previous article, I believe Apple stock needs further catalysts for significant upside. Therefore, for now, I'm sticking with my hold rating.

Growth Moderates But Still Strong

First things first, let's talk about how strong demand was by looking at their top-line results. For revenue, Apple generated a total of $102.466 billion, up 7.94% YoY. You can see above that this is a slowdown of growth from the previous quarter, and while that is undesirable, you should note that FY2025 Q4 still represented the second-highest top-line growth rate in the past three years. Therefore, I would say that overall business activity was relatively robust. Apple was also able to beat analyst expectations by $215.52 million, and that's still quite a decent margin for a company of their size.

If you go and take a look at their earnings release, Apple broke down their net sales by product category and also by geographic area. With the exception of Wearables, Home, and Accessories, they saw YoY growth in every product category as an indication of strength. For iPhone, their most important product, they saw sales of $49.025 billion, up 6.06%. That's quite a deceleration from Q3, but as I'll discuss just below, strong signs for their iPhone 17 will likely help turn this around for FY2026 Q1.

In their breakdown of revenues by region, all geographic areas saw YoY growth with one key exception. Greater China sales dropped from $15.033 billion to $14.493 billion YoY. There was growth in the previous quarter, and so this is potentially concerning. However, it was reported on Nov. 14th that iPhone sales were up 22% YoY in the month after the iPhone 17 launched. This is a stark contrast to the iPhone 16's performance, as sales dropped 5% in the first month after launch in China. This is a good sign for their iPhone sales in general and also good news for their China-specific sales. As of now, it is reported that the iPhone holds a 25% market share in China, and given the brutal competition from companies like Huawei and Xiaomi, that is quite impressive.

Profitability Is On The Rise

As a demonstration of good profitability, Apple reported expansion of both gross and operating margin. As shown in the above chart, gross margin has in fact hit multiyear highs with a reading of 47.18%, and that shows their pricing power remains incredibly strong. Despite the concerns of consumer weakness in the U.S. and globally, Apple seems to have had no problem selling their premium products at full price in this environment.

As for operating margin, there was also some expansion YoY, and you can see that there is some seasonality for this figure. Their total operating expenses increased by 11.38%, which outpaces the revenue growth rate. However, in their breakdown of these expenses, it is shown that R&D spending increased by 14.18%, while SG&A only increased by 8.05%. Therefore, corporate efficiency was actually quite strong. The outsized R&D increase isn't a bad sign, and on the contrary, it may be a good sign given that the company's innovation and AI efforts have been weak in recent years. More on that later.

For Q4, Apple reported an EPS of $1.85. In GAAP terms, that is a major increase from the prior year period's $0.97. However, note that a one-time charge related to the reversal of the European General Court's State Aid decision was recorded in FY2024 Q4, and so this comparison is misleading. Seeking Alpha provides an adjusted view in the chart above. Adjusted growth was at 12.8%, a modest improvement from Q3's 12.14% increase. They were also able to beat bottom line expectations by $0.08. Moving forward, you can see that analysts expect some EPS growth slowdown, which is a bit surprising given that the iPhone 17 has seen strong momentum thus far.

Slowing Share Buybacks

In terms of cash flows, Apple also reported respectable results. FCFs were shown to be $26.49 billion, representing a growth rate of 10.81%. Note that this is the highest growth rate since the year-ago quarter. While that is good news, shareholders may not like the fact that share repurchases dropped significantly YoY. Apple does pay a dividend, but their capital returns are concentrated in the form of stock buybacks. For Q4, they bought back $20.373 billion in stock, down 19.67% YoY. With FCFs increasing by a decent amount, it is a bit surprising that they are slowing their repurchases. There could be many reasons for this, but some potential ones include reserving cash for an AI acquisition or viewing their stock price as less attractive.

Current Events Reaction

Google Gemini Deal

Just around two weeks ago, it was reported that Apple was nearing a deal with Alphabet (GOOGL)(GOOG) to use Gemini for Siri. They are preparing to pay around $1 billion per year for access to the 1.2 trillion parameter Gemini model. I believe this could be a growth driver.

By now, it is no secret that Apple's own AI innovation has been weak, and while users can download AI tools onto their Apple devices, there is some loss of convenience as Siri is more easily accessible. While the iPhone is still a dominant device on the global stage, the lack of AI features puts Apple at a disadvantage to Android.

With this deal, they can quickly raise the level of Siri's intelligence, buying time for them to potentially develop their own AI. Just recently, I wrote an article on Alphabet saying that it was a good deal for them. I believe it's really a win-win, as this deal could also make Apple products more competitive.

Tim Cook To Step Down

Just two days ago (Nov 17th), there were reports that Apple CEO Tim Cook may be stepping down as soon as next year. The company is reportedly ramping up succession planning, and at this point, John Ternus, Senior Vice President of Hardware Engineering, is seen as the most likely candidate to become the next CEO. It was also reported that this transition is "unrelated to Apple's current performance."

Firstly, I would say that Tim Cook has been a strong leader for the company for many years. However, given the company's struggles with innovation and AI development, I believe a new face at the top could help to reignite the company's passion for bleeding-edge technology. Therefore, I view this as a long term positive for shareholders.

Valuation Expands

In my previous article, the forward P/E was at a reading of 30.83 and I stated my belief that the valuation was more or less fair. There has been some multiple expansion since then, as you can see above, but I still believe that the stock is priced correctly.

In today's article some promising signs for the company were discussed. Sales growth is overall solid and strong; iPhone sales in China post iPhone 17 release calm fears regarding a slowdown of their iPhone and China business. Margins also expanded to show good profitability. Last but not least, the current events discussed above have the potential to drive long-term shareholder value.

Therefore, I believe the valuation expansion since my previous article is justified. Like back in August, the stock is at a notable premium to the IT sector, but given investors' perception of the company's stability and dominance, this is not a huge surprise by now.

Concluding Remarks

Apple showed off a respectable Q4 and they have made moves that support the long-term direction of the company and shareholder value. But with the valuation having expanded further and still at a notable premium to the IT sector, I believe more catalysts are needed if the stock is to show significant gains. As stated above, I believe Apple has made the right moves with regard to their Gemini deal and CEO succession, but for the stock to become exciting again, they likely need a considerable jump in innovation or perhaps even a transformative AI acquisition. Therefore, I'm maintaining my hold rating for now.

data.title: Credit Quality And Duration Stress Test: BNDS Might Hold Up In A Market Pullback
data.description:
Corporate spreads sit near multi-year lows while investors increasingly short richly priced credit. Recent U.S. data softening has made corporate debt “most vulnerable” to a slowdown¹.

Lower-rated bonds have begun to lag, and distressed loans have climbed to multi-year highs². Investors sense fragility in credit markets - raising the question: how might the Infrastructure Capital Bond Income ETF (BNDS) perform if conditions tighten?

Active Credit Strategy vs. Passive Benchmarks

Most core bond ETFs track indexes such as the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate, which hold only investment-grade securities like Treasuries, mortgages, and high-grade corporates³. These passive vehicles exclude high-yield debt entirely.

BNDS, by contrast, is actively managed and deliberately credit-focused. Its prospectus allows holdings “of any credit quality,” including high-yield and even distressed bonds⁴.

The strategy seeks income and opportunistic gains through analysis of term, credit, and liquidity premiums⁵. Target sectors often include utilities, REITs, industrials, and pipelines - areas overlooked by traditional aggregate funds. The result is a portfolio with greater corporate exposure and flexibility.

Credit Quality Under Stress

In downturns, lower-rated bonds typically feel the first hit. Credit spreads tend to widen before equity markets react, as risk appetite fades¹. High-yield debt - concentrated in cyclical sectors - can quickly reprice when growth slows². BNDS’s higher-beta credit mix could underperform safer benchmarks during such a pullback.

However, BNDS’s active design allows for tactical defense. Unlike a passive index fund, it can trim weak issuers, hedge exposures, or rotate into improving credits.

In general, active managers can “proactively sell securities with weakening credit quality or buy… bonds poised for an upgrade”⁶. This discretion may help the fund navigate volatility more nimbly than a benchmark-tied alternative.

Managing Duration and Interest-Rate Risk

Interest-rate sensitivity—duration risk - is another critical variable. In rate-driven sell-offs, long-duration bonds often suffer most. BNDS’s team is not anchored to a fixed benchmark duration and can shorten maturities when yields rise.

Active managers “can adjust their exposure to interest-rate risk as the economic climate changes”⁷. That flexibility offers potential cushion against rate shocks.

BNDS can also employ derivatives and hedges to help limit downside exposure⁸. While such tools add complexity, they expand the fund’s defensive playbook compared with index-only vehicles that must absorb market swings directly.

Balancing Risk and Reward

BNDS represents a trade-off: more credit risk than a vanilla bond ETF, but more flexibility to respond to it. Its appeal lies in active management - exiting overpriced securities and repositioning during dislocations⁶. In a market correction, that adaptability could let BNDS capture future value as spreads normalize.

For investors, the key is alignment with objectives. BNDS may underperform core bond ETFs in a sharp risk-off phase, yet its mandate gives it a chance to exploit mispricing once the dust settles. In a bond market where “average” no longer feels safe, active credit selection remains a meaningful differentiator.

Footnotes

  1. Naomi Rovnick, “Debt Market Jitters Signal Caution for High-Flying Stocks,” Reuters, August 12, 2025

  2. Emily Graffeo and Rachel Graf, “Fear Is Coming Back to the Junk Bond Market,” Bloomberg Markets, November 8, 2025

  3. Adam Hayes, “Bloomberg Aggregate Bond Index: Definition and Who Tracks It,” Investopedia, accessed 2025

  4. Infrastructure Capital Advisors, Infrastructure Capital Bond Income ETF (BNDS) Prospectus, Form N-1A (filed Sept. 20, 2024).

  5. Ibid.

  6. Greg Torretti and Joyce Huang, “Why Active ETFs May Make Sense in Today’s Bond Market,” American Century, April 25, 2025

data.title: Principal Well-Being Index: AI's Effect On Staffing
data.description:
By Christian Floro, CFA, CMT, Market Strategist

The latest survey results from the Principal Well-Being Index showed that most small- and medium-sized businesses expect AI to be a growth driver going forward, while only a minority of firms expect it to result in lower staffing and wage levels. This suggests that upskilling and retraining will likely play a significant role in labor markets over time and that AI’s role in supporting employment growth should remain broadly positive and lead to stronger economic growth going forward.

Wage change expectations in the next 12-24 months for firms who are adopting AI

Principal Well-Being Index

The latest Principal Well-Being Index showed that adoption of Artificial Intelligence (AI) technology by businesses has not led to planned decreases in staffing or wages, challenging fears of widespread AI-driven job losses.

The Principal Well-Being Index, now in its twelfth year, tracks sentiment across small- and medium-sized businesses. The latest survey was conducted from September 26 to October 17, gathering responses from 1,000 businesses—comparable in scale to the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index.

AI’s potentially outsized impact on productivity has sparked concerns about widespread layoffs and slower wage growth. However, the survey results indicate the opposite: most firms view AI adoption as a catalyst for growth rather than a threat. Indeed, AI’s perceived long-term impact appears to extend beyond simple labor-cost savings, as AI-induced productivity gains are expected to be the dominant driver behind margin expansion over time.

The survey supports the notion that for those currently employed, upskilling and retraining is more likely than being replaced by AI. Of course, it’s important to keep in mind that as AI adoption remains in early stages, its longer-term net effect on the labor market remains uncertain, especially for entry-level workers fresh out of university. Yet, to the extent AI compliments—rather than substitutes—human capabilities, its role in supporting employment growth should remain broadly positive and lead to stronger economic growth going forward.

Original Post

data.title: Nebius: Why I Am Going All-In
data.description:
AI spending is soaring, setting neo-cloud Nebius Group (NBIS) up for a once-in-a-lifetime revenue and earnings ramp that could result in the company growing its market capitalization many times over in the next couple of years.

The neo-cloud had $146.1 million in revenue in Q325, which calculates to 355% year-over-year growth, thanks to a booming core business of supplying GPU infrastructure to companies. The neo-cloud is on track to build out its data center capacity to 2.5 gigawatts by the end of next year, a 150% increase compared to the company’s prior outlook.

If you have an appetite for risk as well as a conviction that AI will deliver significant growth opportunities in the future, Nebius could be a promising investment for you. Hyperscalers are increasingly willing to open up their wallets for AI-led data center expansion, which makes for a strong upside case for Nebius.

Nebius signed a multi-billion dollar agreement with Microsoft (MSFT) for AI infrastructure, which backs up aggressive growth projections. In addition, Meta (META) is now part of Nebius’ client portfolio. It is my conviction that the current pullback in the market creates a very attractive buying opportunity for Nebius, and if you, like me, believe in the possibilities of AI, the neo-cloud could potentially be a multi-bagger long term.

My Long Thesis for This Undervalued Neo-Cloud

Nebius struck gold in September when it announced a multi-year agreement to provide AI infrastructure capacity to Microsoft. This deal, which has a base value of $17.4 billion, not including a contract upgrade that could deliver up to $19.4 billion in net revenue to Nebius over a five-year period, was a major achievement for Nebius. Initially, this deal catapulted Nebius’ market value to new highs, but the recent pullback could be an opportunity for you to get in at a cheaper price.

Nebius is building out its data center capacity and managed to attract another hyperscaler as well. In addition to Microsoft, Meta signed on to Nebius’ cloud platform and will spend $3 billion over a five-year period on the neo-cloud’s GPUs.

Nebius’ total group revenue in Q325 rose an impressive 355% year-over-year to $146.1 million as companies like Nebius, CoreWeave (CRWV), or IREN Ltd. (IREN) makes aggressive moves into the GPUaaS market.

The agreement between Nebius and Microsoft especially was significant as it accelerates the neo-cloud’s net revenue growth and potentially also shortens the timeline for Nebius to become adjusted EBITDA profitable.

Nebius is still losing money here, though the trajectory shows great promise. In Q325, Nebius’ adjusted EBITDA in the first nine months of the year was negative to the tune of $79.9 million. Though still negative, losses are shrinking, and rapidly so.

Nebius is projecting $7-9 billion in expected annualized run-rate revenue for next year. This follows an earlier projection of $1.0 billion in ARR this year. The increase in the revenue base will be about 7x, which provides fuel not only for net revenue growth but also for a significant upside for the neo-cloud’s market valuation.

Expect to See a One-of-a-Kind Revenue Ramp

Nebius is currently in the process of building out its data centers, stacking them with GPUs, such as Nvidia’s GB200 NVL72 (Blackwell Architecture). Big U.S. companies are still raising their capex forecasts, which is a strong indication that AI-led spending is going to remain a significant catalyst for growth for neo-clouds in the future.

This year, big tech is expected to spend more than $360 billion on artificial intelligence, with companies like Amazon, Meta, and Microsoft leading the way. For 2026, a continuing surge in spending should be expected as NVIDIA (NVDA) gets ready to release its newest-gen GPU, called Rubin.

Additional Upside Linked to Startup Investments

Nebius owns a 28% stake in AI data startup ClickHouse, a developer of open-source columnar databases that are geared towards web analytics. The startup is growing extremely quickly and recently signed up customers like Hewlett Packard and ShopMonkey to its portfolio, surpassing more than 2,000 customers. It is widely expected that the company is going to pursue an IPO in the near- to medium-term future.

Should we see an IPO in the future, Nebius could potentially receive windfall profits from its equity stake in the company. ClickHouse secured $350 million in funding in a Series C round recently, which gives the startup a valuation of $6.4 billion. With a 28% equity stake, worth an estimated $1.7 billion, Nebius could potentially see a liquidity event with regard to ClickHouse and use proceeds for the scaling of its core AI infrastructure segment.

Why I Am Going All-In On The Pull-Back

Investors have woken up to the potential of Nebius’ data center platform, and the Microsoft deal helps the company establish itself as a leading AI infrastructure and GPUaaS business. As of the time of this writing, analysts penciled in $2.87 billion in net revenues for Nebius for 2026, which might actually turn out to be a low estimate, particularly if another customer, like OpenAI, scoops up a deal to secure GPU access.

Nebius just needs one major deal, and the neo-cloud could easily see a significant jump in annual revenue projections going forward. Since OpenAI has made many deals with AI platforms and GPUaaS companies, I would not be surprised if Nebius were to also get a big slice of OpenAI’s spending budget. Nebius’ 2026 price-to-revenue ratio is 7.4x vs. 3.0x for CoreWeave and 14.2x for NVIDIA. Especially relative to Nvidia, Nebius looks undervalued, and you may find the current pullback a decent buying opportunity. Since Nebius has had no issues attracting big clients to its GPU platform, it also seems that the pullback has been exaggerated.

Nebius is growing the fastest in the peer group, and recent net revenue estimates have been revised much higher, a consequence primarily of the Microsoft deal. In my opinion, with Nebius growing so quickly and possibly reaching adjusted EBITDA profitability next year, the GPUaaS company is an attractive investment candidate, with last week’s correction justifying a much more aggressive stance.

Things to Keep in Mind

With demand for AI applications surging, Nebius is in a good position to sign similar deals with other companies, such as OpenAI. Are those deals guaranteed? Of course not. But Nebius has other high-grade customers in its portfolio, the latest one being Meta, that could make it so much easier to attract other high-profile customers as well.

A potential point of contention relates to the durability of capex spending as well as additional share sales in order to fund the scaling of its data centers. A moderating pace of data center buildouts and more share sales to fund capex may counterbalance Nebius' growth upside.

My Verdict

Nebius guided for at least $7.0 billion in revenue (annual recurring) for 2026, pointing to a 7x increase year-over-year. The annual recurring revenue run-rate is likely going to get raised significantly if Nebius makes more deals with other hyperscalers. The adjusted EBITDA trend is highly beneficial, and Nebius has upside derived from its equity stake in ClickHouse.

Hence, I believe Nebius is a promising GPUaaS play for you if AI-led exposure to the data center market is what you are after. While it is true that Nebius is more expensive than CoreWeave, the neo-cloud is seeing strong business trends and secured two major contract wins in a short span of time. The recent pullback is undeserved, as far as I am concerned, and creates an all-in investment opportunity, in my opinion.

data.title: Bitcoin And The Bear
data.description:
We don’t talk much about bitcoin (or other cryptocurrencies) in this space, mostly because this asset class does not have a place in our asset allocation models.

Our advice to clients in this regard is simply to treat cryptos like you would treat any purely speculative wager, be it sports betting or the roulette table in Vegas or whatever else.

In other words, a discretionary activity to undertake outside the guardrails of your long-term financial portfolio with specific objectives for growth and capital protection.

Punters Rise and Fall

Sometimes, though, a discussion about bitcoin is helpful, and this tends to be the case when it serves as a useful proxy for a market environment that has been awash in speculative fever.

As has been the case for much of 2025, ever since traders and other short-term punters dusted off their momentary fears of economic Armageddon in the wake of the April tariff follies. Bitcoin had one heck of a ride, surging by 64 percent from its low on April 8 to a high of $126,315 (per coin) on October 6. But then things went south.

Since that October 6 high, the price of bitcoin has fallen by around 32 percent. Interestingly, bitcoin’s drop started about three weeks before the S&P 500 recorded its last all-time high, on October 28.

Needless to say, that 32 percent reversal is far bigger than what the benchmark equity index has experienced – as of Friday’s market open, the S&P 500 was down 5.1 percent from the October 28 high (stocks are trading more or less flat about an hour into today’s session).

As we noted in our commentary last week, a number of factors are at play here for equities, including AI bubble concerns, the ongoing data fog problem, evidence of a slowdown in consumer spending, and the fragile backdrop of geopolitical instability.

Investors have been particularly unwilling to reward strong earnings reports (e.g., Nvidia (NVDA) numbers) while unleashing their full fury on companies that fall short of their expected numbers.

Cash Flows Persevere

In a market environment dominated by speculation and sometimes mindless momentum, it makes sense that the less fulsome assets are the first to fall. For all that Silicon Valley evangelists (and, more recently, late-to-the-party types from Wall Street) have been hyping the importance of cryptocurrencies as a mainstream asset class, they still collectively have yet to demonstrate a convincing use case in the real economy (apart from facilitating shady transactions on the dark web and, well, speculation for its own sake).

These assets have no value apart from what people trading them perceive that value to be (this is not true for stablecoins, which is a separate discussion entirely and possibly a subject for a future article). The absence of a convincing real-world use case is what keeps cryptocurrencies out of the realm of the asset classes we consider for portfolio construction.

Companies with solid cash flows, manageable debt-to-equity ratios, and believable models for sales and profit growth, on the other hand, will persevere through the periodic downturns that are an inescapable part of long-term investing.

We believe that the most important thing we can tell our clients is to stay disciplined and hold onto their quality assets during the downturns. Trying to guess when to get out is hard; trying to guess when to get back in is even harder and much more likely than not to result in missing out on those important early recovery days after a low point is hit. This discipline is likely to be called on as we head into what could very well be an extremely tricky year ahead.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Meta: Growth Solid, Innovation Costs Mounting
data.description:
My Hold view on Meta Platforms (META) in May 2025 has worked out somehow. It is now down by ~10%, although the S&P 500 is still up by ~12% from the time of my last analysis. Importantly, Meta was up by 20% at some point in August/September, stretching my thesis, before a recent sharp pullback following Q3 results and management commentary.

Despite the pullback now, I feel Meta is not ready to be upgraded to a Buy. The core issue I face in buying Meta now is a lack of conviction in its capital discipline and a defined moat positioning for its futuristic investments. The core business is doing reasonably well, but margins are getting stretched with no clear path for return on investments.

Core Business Reasonably Strong

First up, revenues. Revenues reported in Q3 were actually up by a staggering 26%, and aided by the right channels. Active users were up by 8% YoY. Ad impressions grew by 14%, and the average price per ad also showed double-digit growth. All very good so far. The core business seems to tick the right boxes and is doing reasonably well.

In terms of guidance, the midpoint of $57.5b is still a healthy ~19% YoY uptick in topline. However, seen in the perspective of recent growth levels in the mid-twenties, this seems to be a slight detour from the acceleration in growth seen in the past quarters. In fact, Q4 has been a seasonally bullish month. While that makes for a stiffer comp, it is equally true that the revenue uptick seen in Q4 in the past seems relatively subdued this time around.

There are some other drags in headline numbers too. One time tax adjustments meant a blow for the GAAP EPS. However, normalized numbers beat expectations, so that is not a cause for concern. What impacts the outlook, though, is a $2b increase in expected expenses for the full year from the earlier guided $117b midpoint. Management also highlighted growing regulatory drags in Europe (less personalized ads offered) and youth-related trials in the US in 2026. However, the greater cause for concern on fresh Meta positions lies beyond the revenue guidance and cost increases.

Cost of Building a Moat

At the core of my hesitancy around Meta in May were capital allocation and ROI concerns around a continued investment in Reality Labs and capex as a whole. That story remains unchanged broadly. In fact, market reactions after Q3 results suggest a correction around the same concerns that had me thinking in May.

Reality Labs continues to drag income by $4.4b almost consistently now - a very capital-intensive experimental bet that becomes difficult to value. The inflection to a viable commercial offering is still looking distant. Despite an encouraging ~74% YoY growth in revenues here, the company effectively still burns ~$9.5 for every dollar of revenue generated. The Q4 guidance is not encouraging either. Although the lower Reality Labs revenue in Q4 is more an operational issue (due to the absence of a new VR headset launch in 2025) than a true demand caution, it still could stretch the annual losses in Reality Labs alone to $17-18b. That is meaningfully worse than even what I anticipated in May.

I find the Reality Lab bet more unclear strategically looking at the absence of a 2025 release of the Quest headset. It is unclear how Meta intends to grow this segment, positioning it between spatial computing or the metaverse theme, or either. Ambiguous signals like switching the terminology from metaverse to spatial computing to AI glasses only reinforce my sense that Meta is still probing for a narrative that resonates. Competitors have already captured clearer mindshare. Apple seems to dominate the spatial computing story with Vision Pro, and OpenAI has seized the AI-assistant narrative. The lack of clarity, as far as I am concerned, is disconcerting because losses are piling up at a time when clearer profitability is the need of the hour. Even if Meta generates 20% operating margins from Reality Lab's $100b investments (projected at the current rate of $15b per year) by 2028, it would still need 5-10 years to recover the cumulative costs.

What Reality Labs does to investors like me, is make overall R&D and capital allocation look riskier than they should be. The AI race is still in its initial stages. Investors do flock to bet on capex intensive capability building in Google and Palantir, with the same amount of uncertainties, where Meta's history makes capex increase announcements a cause for caution.

Meta has announced an increased capex spending of $70-72b for the year, not significantly up from the earlier estimates of the $66-72b midpoint. However, Meta has emphasized a continued capex uptick in 2026 to address its growing compute needs. What makes the increased capex spending concerning is a lack of a transparent monetization path. Llama 4 integration across apps is solid progress. Even custom silicon investments (the Rivos acquisition) de-risk Meta from Nvidia dependencies. The AI narrative still seems to be centered around ad and content recommendation improvements, which are already embedded in existing FoA revenues.

Meta's investments in data centers have execution and delay risks. If cloud providers (AWS, Google Cloud, Azure) successfully monetize AI compute through customer access, Meta's captive infrastructure becomes less of a moat. There is no indicative data being discussed yet around utilization rates, inference costs per query, and timeline expectations to measure the potential impact on monetization.

Margin Compression Concerns

The phase of "difficult to measure" investments over operational discipline is already hurting the operating margins, which have receded from ~48% in Q4 2025 to just over 40% now. Structural decay in operating margins due to investments is not something hurting Google or Microsoft because they have lower proportional exposures in moonshots because of their size and structure. I was already wary of margin compression, and there has not been any respite on margins since May. Even the ROIC trajectory has turned south, further fueling the monetization concerns.

Valuations Rightly Pulling Back

The reason why I am still holding Meta instead of selling (given my uncertainty around its investment monetization path) is because its valuations are relatively conducive compared to a Google or a Microsoft, despite the high capex burn. I have used a trailing EV to FCF metric to show the relative valuation comfort in Meta, because it takes into account the heavy capex and losses from Reality Labs. Forward estimates will be less reliable given the uncertain monetization path and timelines. Despite that drag, Meta is trading at ~34x EV to trailing FCF, reasonably lower than Microsoft's ~48x and Google's ~46x mark. The recent corrections post Q3 make this valuation gap more pronounced, making shorts difficult.

At the same time, this valuation opportunity does not imply a buy scenario either. The discount is justified. Meta's lower multiple reflects Reality Labs' losses, AI monetization ambiguity, and margin compression. Critically, forward EV/FCF could be far worse if FCF continues to be suppressed due to capex surges with no clear monetization uplift.​

Not Catching This Falling Knife

The close to 25% corrections from the top are compelling, but Meta's thesis does not provide measurable levers to base a buy thesis on yet. Qualitatively, its moat in the AI race is unclear, and the track record of a Reality Labs bleed is not helping.

If I look beyond fundamentals towards momentum or narrative plays too, this fall is not a great time to invest. Meta has a history of steep corrections. Between the peaks and troughs of 2021-2022, Meta corrected by 75%. Even in the April sell-off earlier this year, a fall of ~33% was seen. Meta is not a knife that should be caught when falling. Even for momentum trades, a 50% retracement from the lows makes for a better trading ploy historically (looking at the last 5 years data), for those concerned about missing out on the cheaper prices today or being able to time a bottom.

As a long-term fundamental investor, I will wait for AI monetization to show up as incremental revenue by mid-2026 or a clear path to be outlined and visible. I would also like Meta to show lower losses in its Reality Labs play (increasing trend currently), and that is both because of its numerical impact and because of getting more confidence around Meta's capital discipline overall. I will also watch for some stability in operating margins before jumping in with fresh capital. Till then, Meta is a hold.

data.title: Selectivity Drives Outperformance In Europe
data.description:
By Rich Hill, Global Head of Real Estate Research & Strategy

Open-ended core fund returns gain momentum

The European ODCE index posted net cash returns of +0.83% (local currency), marking a sixth straight positive quarter and accelerating from +0.24% in 2Q. Capital returns turned positive at +0.08%, the third positive performance in the past four quarters. Still, performance remains below the +1.51% posted in 1Q25 and +0.94% in 4Q24.

Across the Atlantic, the U.S. ODCE index returned +0.52% in 3Q25, with income returns of +0.79% offset by negative capital returns of -0.26%. Historically, the U.S. ODCE index has outperformed in 71% of quarters since 3Q11, with annualized returns of +6.4% versus +3.3% in Europe. Yet, since early 2023, performance has reversed, with Europe outperforming in eight out of eleven quarters.

Quarterly total returns of European vs. U.S. real estate funds

3Q 2011–3Q 2025

The recent outperformance of Europe is consistent with our view that the continent may benefit from improving economic conditions, wider cap rates, and lower sovereign debt yields making leverage more accretive. Indeed, as of 2Q25, Europe’s unlevered asset level total returns across stood +6.9% over the trailing four quarters compared to +4.7% for the U.S. over the same period. These results are consistent with our midyear forecast of 6–7% total returns for Europe in 2025, versus roughly ±5% in the U.S.

Another layer to the story

Macro tailwinds alone don’t explain the divergence. Return dispersion across funds has also widened. Over the past year, the median net return for European ODCE funds was +4.2%, with top-quartile managers at +5.3% and bottom-quartile managers at +2.5%.

U.S. ODCE dispersion is even starker: top-quartile funds delivered 1-year annualized gross total returns of +5.7% vs. 4.9% for the second quartile, 4% for the third quartile, and a notably weak 0.3% for the bottom quartile. In other words, the top U.S. funds are outperforming, but laggards are far underperforming their European peers.

Focused strategies continue to outperform diversified

The broader INREV All-Fund Index delivered a stronger +1.05% total return, supported by income (+0.67%) and capital growth (+0.38%). This is consistent with historical trends, as the All-Fund Index has outperformed the European ODCE index in nearly 74% of quarters since 3Q11, driven in part by stronger single-sector performance.

Single-sector funds continued to outpace multi-sector peers (+0.55% total return), with the notable exception of office. Sector-level returns included:

  • Residential: +1.87% (+1.15% capital growth)
  • Industrial: +1.41% (+0.80% income growth)
  • Retail: +0.96% (+0.66% income growth)
  • Office: +0.27% (0.50% income growth)

Comparison of European fund returns

3Q25 vs. trailing 4-quarter total

Bottom line

European real estate markets continue to demonstrate steady momentum, with consistent performance supported by improving macro conditions and tighter return dispersion. While U.S. funds still lead at the top end of the return spectrum, European strategies are benefiting from greater stability across managers and sectors. In the current environment, where macro tailwinds and manager skill both matter, selectivity both in geography and strategy remains the key to capturing outperformance.

Original Post
