data.title: Tenaris Q3 And 2025E Update: Continued Risk, Especially With Market Downside
data.description:
I have been covering Tenaris (TS) for a few years at this point. It is one of those businesses where I have been able to do a good entry and a good rotation of my position back before April of 2025. The company then significantly declined and has been under pressure for some time. If you followed my targets, then you probably reinvested or at least considered as much in the last 6 months.

The recent report and market trends saw the company bounce back up to the level where I previously sold. This makes the company a good RoR for those who bought cheap before, but it does not make it, to my mind, a good investment for new investors unless fundamentals and upside have changed.

And that is what we are going to look at here.

Tenaris has, in my view, a mix of valuation and results problems (meaning recent results). The company has cut its forecasts, and analysts have cut their forecasts for the company as well. The volatility in the market is something to be expected given the company's exposure.

Generally, I have found straight oil and energy companies to be better investments than something like this - both in terms of yield and potential upside. Tenaris and companies like it serving the oil industry are investable at the right yield and upside. The recent 3Q25 came out at the end of October, about 3 weeks ago from the time of writing, and it is these results we will look at to determine what price you should pay for an energy service company in a market that seems set to go negative (judging by recent trends and this week's ups and downs) in the near or medium term.

Tenaris has a lot going for it. I like its dividend, its Luxembourg domicile, and its APAC and African exposure. It trades and operates in an overall tricky market, wedged between low-cost producers and operators/customers demanding cheaper products, while being inherently a premium product company (in a segment increasingly moving towards commoditization).

This requires careful consideration.

Tenaris - Upside is there, but at this price, it is a tricky one

Positives first, including positives for 3Q25 and for the coming full-year results. This is not a company in any immediate danger. The fact that it has a net cash position of $3.5B at the end of 3Q25 should count for something - even if overall 3Q25 results were not necessarily all that great.

The company's business idea is a solid and simple one. It operates in a segment where its competition consists of companies in straight steel industry, like ArcelorMittal (MT), which also cover, but also smaller ones like Aperam. In straight oil service, we can look at Halliburton and SLB (SLB), also Baker Hughes (BKR). As we will see in the valuation portion of the article, one of the problems now facing Tenaris is that peer valuation levels are simply a lot more attractive here than they once were, and relative to Tenaris, could be an argument for investing before Tenaris itself.

3Q25 was okay but not great. Net sales decreased (but overall were decent, especially the increase in welding), but operating income was up somewhat, and FCF decreased to less than $135M due to WC increase and high inventory levels. Tenaris is one of the more significant Tariff-impacted companies that I cover - and the full flow of tariff impacts has not yet come to the company either.

So there are headwinds left, which leads me to believe that this recent surge is going to see a downturn again.

Primary global energy demand sees no decline or weakness that would make Tenaris a bad company to invest in at the right price. Despite renewable investments, the level of CapEx in E&P remains elevated and is likely to remain elevated. This is one of the primary indicators I track for how these companies do, and for the full year, we saw an increase, with a slowed growth rate from 2023.

I expect either a slight increase or flat trends in 2025E as well, with one exception being Brazil and Petrobras, where I believe the CapEx will be increasing further.

The risk profile for Tenaris remains as before - high-end product sales will never return to 2014 levels, when the entire market for those products turned down (the deep-water offshore customer/order decline), and combined with one of the closest peers pushing into low-cost production, we are likely to see further pricing pressure. How I consider this is that margins are unlikely to grow from here - the company will have to grow by volume, and recent trends tell us that this seems somewhat unlikely. Especially since the US shale segment has moved from Premium to semipremium products, which is (combined with tariffs) what is causing pain here. Shale was, and arguably still is, Tenaris' most important end market. Recent results also highlight slowing drill activity in key markets, importantly, both Canada and the US.

Volatility in the macro is coming from China's continued level of increased export of steel. The EU is applying protectionist measures, as is the US.

One of the more important potential upsides in 3Q25 was the election in Argentina, which implies that there is now a place for foreign capital for development projects, which would require products from companies like Tenaris. This pro-investment climate is likely to trickle down to this company as well, in assets like Vaca Muerta, with an expected number of rigs operating in Argentina.

Whenever the rig count goes up, and Tenaris is there, you can expect upside. Further upside potential is from further share buybacks. Remember, Tenaris has a controlling family, and this changes these considerations somewhat. Tenaris still has $600m out of the original $1.2B worth of buybacks in its announcement, and the company has confirmed its intention to fully execute that second tranche of buybacks. This will likely also support the share price and valuation growth.

My forecast for Tenaris is slow growth or flat development. In 2025E, I expect a slight decline from 2024, as the overall growth level for CapEx moves down, and coupled with increased competition and tariffs, I do not believe a return to the $12.5B+ level of sales revenue until 2028E at the earliest. Same with EBIT/EBITDA - I expect it to be flat from today's levels, about $2.8B annualized in the next 3-4 years. The upside for investors will come from internal improvements and buybacks, which require (in my view) investors to buy shares at a lower valuation to ensure that underperformance is an unlikely outcome. Current GAAP forecasts confirm this and are in line with my own.

The company's advantages, including Rig Direct and the new Bay Mills asset, will work for the company and increase earnings and lower tariff impacts, with US-based production - but I view this as overall unlikely to fully offset the negative impact of increased competition, flattening out CapEx potential, and more stable than significantly growing demand.

That makes this, at heart, a pure valuation play and little else.

The valuation for the company is currently as follows.

Valuation for Tenaris - Upside possible, but better at lower multiples.

The company lacks a moat, despite its Rig Direct program. Its assets related to premium production in the OCTG sector are a competitive advantage, not a moat in my assessment. Differentiation here is minimal; aside from that, premiumization. The volatility in the end markets can be expressed with the example of the Vallourec/Tenaris decline back in the shale boom of -07 to -14, and the result is a premiumized OCTG market that is down to less than one-third of demand.

Regardless of how you view or "like" the company, this is something to keep in mind.

Tenaris' appeal depends on how you view its valuation. As you know from my work, I tend toward conservatism when it comes to applying multiples. So when the company in question has a 5-year multiple of X, I tend to listen (especially if that aligns with the longer-term multiples). For Tenaris, that multiple is 9.9x. The company currently trades at 11x. Based on the likelihood for earnings decline or flat development (see above), and the current forecast of less than 0.2% AEPS growth on average for the next 3 years (which, based on trends, I agree with), I cannot see a good argument for applying any higher multiple than 12-13 on this company - and even that is stretching it.

We are talking about valuing a company that reports little to no growth while in an inflated market where it competes with premium products in an industry that has shown a liking for sub-premium. The volatility in forecast accuracy can be confirmed by looking at trends, with less than 10% accuracy over the past 10 years, with about a 50/50 chance to either beat or miss those targets. Not the best track record, nor the best set of expectations for a good performance here.

It is this sort of trend that ensures that even at a relatively high 12x P/E forecast, the company does not even generate 10% annualized RoR, even with its 4%+ yield included.

Based on this, the company would either need to grow earnings significantly more than expected, which, based on the macro and sector backdrop, I consider to be unlikely, or trade at a higher premium, which, again, based on company growth, I consider to be very unlikely. Beyond that, I do not really see much chance of this company generating better returns.

Then we include the context of the per valuation. SLB (SLB) is a company I have not extensively covered beyond peripheral mentions in other articles, but it trades more attractively relative to its historicals, has a 3%+ yield, and has an A credit rating and lower debt than Tenaris. It has an upside to a 13-14x P/E of 18% annualized, and for this company, that is a discount. Halliburton (HAL) has a 15%+ annualized upside to a P/E of below 13x, with a BBB+ credit, and a lower yield. Also, more attractive than Tenaris here.

Based on the above-presented outlooks and estimates, I consider Tenaris to be a "HOLD". On more conservative growth estimates, I shift my PT back down to below €16/share, with a fair value of €20/share for a 15% annualized upside to a conservative estimate. For the ADR TS, this comes to $37/share, given that this is a 2x ADR.

Risks & Considerations

The negatives for the company, I believe, will ensure that we will never see the valuations that are causing the lofty targets we see here. The sales of high-end products were at truly massive levels before 2014 – it is unlikely this will ever happen again. These are the primary risks.

Rivals are moving to low-cost commoditized production of products, which will be interesting for producers in areas like Brazil and China, and there has been an entry into Tenaris segments with local US OCTG producers as well, which represents the secondary risk to the company. Beyond this, there are macro and other factors over which the company lacks controlling influence, but that are nonetheless worth considering.

That means my thesis for the company is as follows.

Thesis

Tenaris is a leading company in the attractive segment of energy services. The company has a leading position in several markets and has one of the better balance sheets in the segment and industry, with extremely low leverage and high overall interest coverage.

Tenaris may not have the highest yield or even a high yield for this sort of company, but it has a history of outperforming the market over time. Investors who bought 20 years ago have done very well for themselves with over 12% annualized return, inclusive of dividends - this is despite the downcycle lows we saw in 2016, and that makes the company a potentially attractive opportunity here.

I consider the company to be a "HOLD" below a share price of €16/share, lowered from my last article, making it unattractive here. This translates to an ADR PT for ticker TS of $37/share.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. Based on this and a valuation target of €16/share, where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that is attractive enough for me, I give the company a "Hold" rating as of November 2025. The company fulfills an insufficient number of my fundamental criteria for investment.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Brightstar Lottery: Forecasting Three Years Of Cash, Dividends, And Buybacks
data.description:
A fat special dividend in July, a mountain of buybacks, and a roadmap stretching years ahead—that’s what you’d be sitting on if you’d followed my ‘Buy’ call back in March.

Two out of my three pieces since then have even beaten the S&P.

Not bad at all!

We’ve tracked everything from the IGT handoff to Brightstar Lottery (BRSL) to the consortium locking in the LottoItalia renewal, even though a lot of people expected the license to stay with Flutter (FLTR)/Sisal.

And here we are again, heading into another quarter.

To be honest, the market didn’t take the Q3 FY 2025 results all that well. The stock’s down about 9% since they came out on November 4th with mixed results out of the hat.

At this point, the stock is indicating an implied upside of ~23% to the Wall St. consensus target and at least 42% to mine at $22. But like I've been saying, the upside (tempting as it looks) isn't the whole picture.

This quarter, they bumped the dividend by 10% ($0.22 per share) and came right out and said they’ll hand back $1.7 billion to shareholders out of the $4 billion they pulled in from the sale by FY 2028.

We’re dealing with a straight-up cash cow here.

I'll sketch out the model right after we run through the quarter, just so you get a real feel for how much shareholder yield should be on the table going forward.

Jackpots Are Back

Brightstar's revenue had been taking a hit the last two quarters thanks to softer comparable sales at the big jackpot spots in the U.S., but that hangover didn't show up this time around.

It's worth keeping in mind we're up against very tough comps.

FY 2024 was packed with jackpots, and that pushed betting sharply higher (Q2 alone saw comparable sales climb about 22.5%). And, since Brightstar earns a percentage of recurring revenue from these bets (in addition to LMAs, of course), it ends up benefiting from these accumulation periods.

This quarter, Powerball topped out at a $1.8 billion jackpot, making it the second-biggest in U.S. lottery history.

So, you can imagine that many rushed to place their bets.

For Brightstar, that translated into a 69.2% jump in comparable sales year over year, enough to wipe out the double-digit declines from the first half, although comps are still down 10.5% year to date. Instant sales were up 1.6% year over year, a bit faster than what we saw in the previous quarter.

Overall, comparable sales in the United States remained virtually flat year-over-year.

Looking at Italy and the rest of the world (places like the Czech Republic, Barbados, and the Caribbean lotteries) comparable sales kept climbing at a solid pace. In Italy, tweaks like the new Miliardario and the push toward pricier €25 tickets helped speed things up even more.

You can see the bigger picture here:

Total revenue came in at $629 million, up 7.3% from a year ago. For Brightstar, this was the strongest quarterly revenue growth in at least two years. And the funny thing? Even with that kind of number, they still came in at least $4.8 million short of what Wall St. was looking for.

Maybe folks just got carried away with the jackpot story? Could it be? But to me, this was still a strong lift in revenue.

Here’s how each piece fed into the quarter’s revenue:

Despite gross margin slipping a bit from last year because of higher POP printing system rollout costs, Brightstar still managed to lift its bottom line thanks to lighter SG&A and lower interest expenses.

Adjusted EBITDA, for example, went from $264 million to $294 million (an 11.3% jump), and net income rose 18.6% to $117 million.

Below I pulled together the recent margins so we can look at them side by side:

Italian Lotto and Temporary Pressure on Cash Flow

Cash flow is where folks usually get confused, especially if Brightstar’s model isn’t something you look at every day.

Since a large part of their revenue comes from long-term lottery contracts, they have to pay those license fees upfront. Because that counts as an operating expense, it drags OCF into the red even when the business is doing just fine.

Just look at this quarter, and you will understand.

OCF came in at –$446 million, mainly because they paid $579 million to the Italian Customs and Monopolies Agency—just a slice of the €2.23 billion they still owe under the license.

But does this mean they are terrible cash generators? Not at all.

Adjusted for that payment, Brightstar would’ve put up about $573 million in OCF and as much as $334 million in FCF.

It's still very much a cash cow.

All of this became possible thanks to the roughly $4 billion they pulled in from the widely discussed sale of IGT Digital Gaming.

To make it easier for you to visualize what they've done with that money so far, here it is:

Going forward, there are two more payments on the way: about €300 million (likely hitting in Q4 FY 2025) and another €1.4 billion in the first half of FY 2026. Both will keep putting temporary pressure on Brightstar’s cash flow.

Probably, to be able to pay this, they will stretch net leverage to up to 3x EBITDA, as in the long-term target. As they have $2.7 billion in net debt on the balance sheet and they are targeting an adjusted EBITDA of $1.1 billion, we could expect it to end up at $3.3 billion (adding at least another $600 million by the first half of next year).

Great information, good to consider in any model we do going forward, isn't it?

But you might be wondering if running the Italian Lottery is really worth that kind of money. And I’ll tell you straight up—it is.

In the past nine years, the Italian Lotto has had roughly €8 billion in annual sales, which is a massive amount by any standard. Keep in mind they get 6% on wagers plus another 8% on iLottery bets, so you can figure the consortium is raking in at least €480 million a year.

Dividends: Are They Still Protected?

We received a ton of information in the last earnings call (a ton, really!), and one piece of it was that dividends would increase by 10% to $0.22 per share quarterly.

That's $0.88 per share annually, a pretty hefty yield of 5.6%.

Doing some simple math, if we assume they normalize buybacks to low single digits every year from now on, Brightstar would be distributing ~$165.5 million annually in dividends.

Is that sustainable? Well, let's see.

Since they are expecting EBITDA to be $1.1 billion for FY 2025 and to grow steadily at a CAGR of 3% until FY 2028, I'm targeting $1.13 billion for FY 2026. Knowing they're aiming for an EBITDA-to-OCF conversion of about 70%, OCF should land somewhere around $800 million in FY 2026. Another piece of information is that Capex will likely remain at $400 million.

We know that the largest chunk of the Italian Lottery payout (~$1.6 billion already converted) will be collected in FY 2026 and will reduce OCF to -$800 million (-$1.2 billion FCF after deducting Capex).

I figure that by FY 2026 they'll probably put part of that cash pile to work (the nearly $1.6 billion they've got sitting around) and add a bit of debt on top. But nothing that would push leverage past their 3x EBITDA target.

They'd be able to claw back those $165.5 million. It wouldn't be painless, but they could get there.

In a normal year like FY 2027, they could probably even increase it given the already normalized FCF of $412 million, covering it by almost 2.5x.

There’s one more move they’ve laid out: $1.7 billion back to shareholders between FY 2025 and FY 2028. So far, they’ve handed out about $900 million, leaving around $800 million still to go.

After the fast-paced buyback last quarter (nearly 6% fewer shares than a year ago), they’ve got another $250 million left under their biannual buyback plan.

Adding it all up, if dividends remain stable over these three years and the entire authorization is carried out, we'd be talking about a total return of $746.5 million, $53.5 million less than they stated.

This smells like more dividend increases on the way. Probably something in the mid-single digits starting in FY 2027, when FCF is no longer under so much pressure.

How Much Can You Expect in Returns?

As I told you before, I continue to expect dividends to deliver mid-single-digit returns annually until FY 2028 (perhaps mid-to-high towards the end of this period).

For share buybacks, I believe they will start flowing more strongly in FY 2027.

So you're basically looking at a mid-single-digit shareholder yield in FY 2026, and then high single-digit returns in FY 2027 and FY 2028. Given what they've laid out so far, that feels like a pretty realistic path.

Bottom Line

In addition to those hefty returns coming from a low-risk model built on recurring revenue and long-term contracts, I also found it interesting that Brightstar pointed out how lottery spending tends to hold up even during recessions.

This is something I was aware of, but I didn't have any graphs of my own.

Note that in the last three major recessions (dotcom bust, the GFC, and even Covid), lottery revenues didn’t crack. They held steady or went up in U.S. From 2020 to 2021, they even increased enough to push the CAGR to 6.7% by 2022.

But it’s not just the returns or the resilience that catch my eye. The multiples are low right now too.

If we take the projected adjusted EBITDA of $1.1 billion for FY 2025 (perhaps slightly less when we unadjust it), we would conclude that the stock is trading at a multiple of ~5x to 6x EBITDA, well below the 5-year average of 7.3x.

You could argue, quite rightly, that after the sale of the Digital division, the market re-rated the stock to adjust it to a slower growth scenario.

But I don't think Brightstar deserved such a low multiple. After all, it's the world's largest pure-play lottery company.

Peers like The Lottery Corporation (OTCPK:LTRCF), ZEAL, and Intralot (OTCPK:IRLTF) are trading at much higher multiples. Pollard Banknote (PBL:CA), which also runs charity games on top of lotteries, is closer to Brightstar, though its 5-year average sits around 9x EBITDA.

In my opinion, 7x EBITDA is fair for Brightstar while it still has its FCF under pressure.

This would give us a target very close to the $22 we were aiming for before.

Similarly, if we model a DCF with that FCF path I mentioned earlier ($1.2 billion in FY 2026, stabilizing at $400 million until FY 2029, when it increases to ~$500 million), we arrive at a very similar result.

And keep in mind that I'm considering a low perpetual growth rate (1.5% annually) and 8.5% to discount it to present value.

That being said, with an upside of ~42%, I'll reiterate my 'Strong Buy' rating here and expect those hefty returns I mentioned.
